did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including DenseNet201, ResNet50, and EfficientNet-B0, to perform automated binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma on dermoscopic images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and comparison of three deep learning models (DenseNet201, ResNet50, EfficientNet-B0) on a public dataset of 10,000 dermoscopic images. It details specific methodology (transfer learning, preprocessing, metrics) and reports new experimental results (e.g., DenseNet201 achieving 90.80% accuracy). Although published in a conference proceedings (ICSTSN), it contains original research findings rather than being a narrative or systematic review.

title: Automated Classification of Skin Cancer Using DenseNet201, ResNet50 and EfficientNet-B0 Deep Learning Models on Dermoscopic Images
journal: 2025 Fourth International Conference on Smart Technologies and Systems for Next Generation Computing (ICSTSN)
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep learning framework for the automated binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma using dermoscopic images. The researchers evaluated three Convolutional Neural Network architectures—DenseNet201, ResNet50, and EfficientNet-B0—on a publicly accessible dataset of 10,000 images. The models were trained using transfer learning and standardized preprocessing techniques, including data augmentation and normalization. Performance was assessed using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. The results demonstrated that DenseNet201 significantly outperformed the other models, achieving a test accuracy of 90.80% and a test loss of 0.2420. The authors conclude that DenseNet201 is a highly effective tool for aiding dermatologists in early melanoma detection due to its superior gradient flow and feature reuse capabilities.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using a dataset of 10,000 dermoscopic images.
The comparator class in the binary classification task, representing benign lesions in the dataset.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma (benign lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'automated melanoma classification' and 'binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma)' using dermoscopic images. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma or non-melanoma) using Binary Cross-Entropy loss. Future work mentions extending to multi-class classification, but the current study is strictly binary classification.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly accessible dataset containing 10,000 dermoscopic images of benign and malignant skin lesions, used in this study for binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma.

use.training: The dataset was split into training and testing sets in an 80:20 ratio. The training portion was used to train the DenseNet201, ResNet50, and EfficientNet-B0 models using transfer learning and data augmentation.
use.testing: The testing portion (20% of the dataset) was used to evaluate the performance of the models, reporting test accuracy and loss.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, loss) without statistical significance testing.

Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: DenseNet201 outperformed ResNet50 and EfficientNet-B0. The dataset was split into training and testing sets in an 80:20 ratio. The study focused on binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). No other specific metric values (Precision, Recall, F1-Score) were reported numerically in the text, though they were listed as evaluation metrics.

architecture_used.pretraining: ImageNet-pretrained weights used for transfer learning

architecture_details.DenseNet201: Densely connected topology to reduce vanishing gradients and promote feature reuse
architecture_details.ResNet50: Residual connections (skip connections) to mitigate vanishing gradients
architecture_details.EfficientNet-B0: Compound scaling technique to balance accuracy and computational efficiency

preprocessing: Data augmentation (random rotations, flipping, zooming, brightness modifications)
training_details.hyperparameters: Batch size, number of epochs, and initial learning rate adjusted experimentally (specific values not reported)

preprocessing_techniques: Downsizing images to 224x224 pixels to comply with input specifications of ResNet50, DenseNet201, and EfficientNet-B0
Normalization of pixel intensities to the interval [0,1] to speed up convergence
Data augmentation including random rotations, flipping, zooming, and brightness modifications to increase variety and reduce overfitting

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during training to increase data variety and reduce overfitting. Specific probability or angle ranges were not reported.
Applied during training to increase data variety and reduce overfitting. Specific probability or axis were not reported.
Applied during training to increase data variety and reduce overfitting. Specific zoom ranges were not reported.
Applied during training to increase data variety and reduce overfitting. Specific intensity ranges were not reported.

conclusions: DenseNet201 significantly outperformed ResNet50 (76.03%) and EfficientNet-B0 (68.64%), achieving the highest test accuracy of 90.80% and a test loss of 0.2420 on the binary classification of melanoma versus non-melanoma.

DenseNet201's superior performance is attributed to its densely connected topology, which enhances gradient flow, reduces vanishing gradients, and promotes feature reuse, making it effective for intricate medical image processing.

EfficientNet-B0 demonstrated the lowest performance (68.64% accuracy), suggesting that its baseline variation may lack sufficient representational capacity for this challenging task, and its use in clinical settings with limited resources is limited by its sacrifice in computing efficiency.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding generalizability, noting that the dataset may not accurately reflect the wide range of skin tones, lesion presentations, and imaging situations encountered in actual clinical practice.
The scope was limited to binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma), which does not fully represent the real-world dermatological task of distinguishing between numerous lesion types.

Future work will focus on expanding the framework to multi-class lesion classification, incorporating more diverse and representative datasets, and utilizing explainable AI techniques like Grad-CAM to improve interpretability and clinical credibility.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single public Kaggle dataset (10,000 images) without external validation, limiting generalizability to diverse clinical populations.
The authors explicitly acknowledge that the dataset may not accurately reflect the wide range of skin tones, lesion presentations, and imaging situations encountered in actual clinical practice.
The scope is limited to binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma), which does not represent the real-world dermatological task of distinguishing between numerous lesion types.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance comparisons were based solely on descriptive metrics.
Specific hyperparameter values (batch size, number of epochs, initial learning rate) were adjusted experimentally but not reported, hindering reproducibility.
Data augmentation techniques (rotations, flipping, zooming, brightness) were applied, but specific probabilities or ranges were not reported.
The paper does not address potential data leakage risks or class imbalance issues within the Kaggle dataset, which are common challenges in skin cancer detection.

EfficientNet-B0 showed notably low performance (68.64% accuracy), suggesting potential limitations of baseline variations for this specific task, though no ablation studies were provided to confirm this.
The study lacks explainability analysis (e.g., Grad-CAM) despite mentioning it as future work, which is critical for clinical trust and deployment.
